validation.lecture: CMU 15-721 Lecture 09: Join Algorithms (Skew) + Lecture 14: Parallel Execution
validation.concept: Partition imbalance from data skew causes straggler threads; COUNT DISTINCT is worst case
validation.proof: West partition = 90.0% of rows vs expected 20.0% (4.5x imbalance); Simple agg: uniform=83ms, skewed=94ms (1.14x); Heavy agg: uniform=523ms, skewed=611ms (1.17x)
validation.validates: L09 claim: hash join on skewed key sends 90% of rows to one partition; parallel execution is only as fast as the slowest partition (straggler); COUNT DISTINCT forces full dedup of the large partition in memory
validation.interpretation: With 90% of rows in 'West', one thread processes 4.5x more data than expected. Simple aggregation (SUM, COUNT) slows by 1.14x — the West partition dominates hash table insertions. Heavy aggregation (COUNT DISTINCT, STDDEV, P95) slows by 1.17x because COUNT DISTINCT must dedup 90.0% of all customer IDs in a single hash set, creating memory pressure. L09 solution: 'partial aggregation + shuffle' or 'broadcast small table'; Spark AQE detects this skew and splits the West partition.

maps_to: CMU 15-721 Lecture 09: Join Algorithms (Skew), Lecture 14: Parallel Execution
